one_line_takeaway: A concise Zscaler survey that maps LLM interaction-layer threats (prompt injection, jailbreaking, backdoors, data poisoning) to their defenses, useful as an entry-point taxonomy but offering no original experiments or comparative evaluation.
key_finding: The recurring theme is a persistent 'shield and spear' asymmetry: interaction-layer attacks keep evolving and current defenses trade off security against model utility. Concretely, the survey reports (from cited works) that automated jailbreak generation and clean-label prompt-based backdoors reach high success rates while remaining hard to detect, and that defenses such as data filtering or reducing model capacity give only minimal protection against data poisoning at the cost of reduced accuracy. The organizing insight is that attacks needing only I/O access are the practically important ones for real deployments.
core_contribution: A survey (not an experimental paper) that organizes LLM security threats into a two-branch taxonomy and pairs each attack class with its defenses. Branch 1, Prompt Hacking, covers Prompt Injection and Jailbreaking; Branch 2, Adversarial Attacks, covers Backdoor Attacks and Data Poisoning. The authors (Liu and Hu, Zscaler) motivate the split by access model: they focus on attacks that only touch the input/output interaction layer, so they apply to both open- and closed-source LLMs, explicitly excluding gradient/architecture-access attacks as infeasible against proprietary models. The paper's own contribution is the structuring and synthesis of ~23 cited works into this attack/defense mapping plus two illustrative GPT-4o role-play jailbreak figures; it introduces no new method, dataset, or benchmark.
threat_model: Adversary interacts with a deployed, instruction-tuned LLM through its input/output interface (black-box-friendly), or, for training-time attacks, can inject data into uncurated/external training or fine-tuning corpora. Goals surveyed: bypassing safety controls to elicit restricted content (jailbreaking), overriding the model's original instructions (prompt injection), and planting hidden triggers that cause targeted malicious behavior while behaving normally otherwise (backdoor/data poisoning). The survey frames attacks as universally applicable because they exploit the common interaction layer rather than model internals; training-time attacks additionally assume a compromised data supply chain or untrusted model source.
limitations: As a survey it presents no original evaluation, no common benchmark, and no head-to-head comparison of the defenses it lists, so relative effectiveness cannot be judged from the paper itself. Coverage is narrow: it deliberately excludes gradient-based/white-box attacks (e.g., GCG-style optimization) and largely omits privacy-extraction, model-stealing, agentic/tool-use, and RAG-specific threats. Cited numbers are heterogeneous (different models, datasets, and metrics) and are not normalized. It is a 2024 (v1) preprint 'under review' with only ~23 references, so it is a compact primer rather than a comprehensive taxonomy.

quantitative_results: MasterKey (Deng et al., cited): automated jailbreak prompts achieved a 21.58% success rate vs 7.33% for existing prompts
PAIR (Chao et al., cited): often jailbreaks a target LLM in fewer than twenty queries
ProAttack (Zhao et al., cited): clean-label prompt-trigger backdoor reaching nearly 100% attack success rate on text classification tasks
Composite Backdoor Attack / CBA (Huang et al., cited): 100% attack success rate on LLaMA-7B with minimal impact on model accuracy
Scope figure: survey synthesizes roughly 23 cited references
baselines_compared: Survey conducts no experiments and compares no baselines of its own; it qualitatively contrasts attack methods (e.g., MasterKey automated vs manual prompts) and defense families (prevention vs detection; white-box Fine-mixing/E-PUR/CUBE vs black-box RAP/ONION/MDP) only as reported in the cited primary works
